evidence_quotes.quote: owners and users of video games against planned (and unannounced) obsolescence
You and UBISOFT (or its licensors) may terminate this EULA, at any time, for any reason.

paragraphs.text: I seemed very enriching and valuable for the proposal for the Digital Fairness Act to add to it a clause protecting owners and users of video games against planned (and unannounced) obsolescence to which they are subject. It is the case that publishers of video games (publishers, as they are known in English) have long been taking advantage of the lack of regulation and legal definitions surrounding the video game as a commercial product, while at the same time using irrelevant and invalid excuses about the hardship cost of maintaining a video game when it is no longer replaceable, in order to return it, literally, to always be inaccessible to anyone, including, of course, its very owner. This is what Stop Killing Games’ campaign seeks to raise awareness. It is not a question of asking publishers to maintain the online service of a video game indefinitely (many of them cannot even be performed without being connected to the internet, even if the game itself, by its nature, does not even require it): this means that access to a previously acquired product or service is not artificially impossible. It should also be noted that this practice is likely to violate Council Directive 93/13/EEC of 5 April 1993 on unfair terms in consumer contracts, as end-user licensing agreements (EULA) display terms similar to those of Ubisoft: You and UBISOFT (or its licensors) may terminate this EULA, at any time, for any reason. Termination by UBISOFT will be effective upon (a) notice to You or (b) termination of Your UBISOFT Account (if any) or (c) at the time of UBISOFTs decision to discontinue offering and/or supporting the Product. [...] Upon termination for any reason, You must immediately uninstall the Product and destroy all copies of the Product in Your possession. (Source: https://www.ubisoft.com/legal/documents/eula/en-US). It is not mentioned at any time that discontinuing or stopping the game would lead to the prevention of access to the game or until it is destroyed. However, this is exactly what they have done with the ‘The Crew’ video game (see https://stopkillinggames.wiki.gg/wiki/Dead_game_list) and what they undeniably require the owners to carry out (physical and digital) copies of their video games.
